researchNotes: Math topics don't decompose into discrete trivia facts the way General Studies does — the same handful of techniques (BODMAS order, fraction-of-decimal, ratio scaling, simultaneous equations, algebraic-identity shortcuts) get re-tested every year with different numbers. So the reading material teaches each technique once with a fully worked example (8 techniques, all kept — no real overlap to dedupe), and the mock test applies the *same* techniques to fresh numbers (12 problems) rather than reusing the reading numbers verbatim, so genuine application is tested, not memorized arithmetic. Two reading items and one mock item are anchored to real sourced past-paper numbers; the rest are standard authored drill problems for these same patterns — flagged transparently via sourceTag rather than dressed up as past-paper hits they aren't. APPEND-ONLY 2026-07 pass (this topic is also used on Day 1, already completed by the user — existing r01-r08/m01-m10 IDs and content untouched): re-scanned the OCR bank with 'simplify/BODMAS' keywords and found one genuine algebraic-factoring past-paper question (image-verified, difference-of-squares-style cancellation), then added standard authored patterns not yet covered (sum/difference of cubes, continued fractions, LCM fraction addition, surds) to round out technique coverage.

reading.intro: Simplification is 1 of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills (15 questions total for the whole Aptitude unit). TNPSC's simplification questions are rarely 'plug into one formula' — they combine BODMAS, fractions/decimals, ratios, and algebraic shortcuts. Each item below is a worked method, not just an answer — the goal is to recognize which technique a given expression calls for.

qa.question: Method — BODMAS with a nested bracket and an unknown: Solve for x: 57 − [42 − {37 − (20 − x)}] = 176 ÷ (8 ÷ 2)
qa.answer: Work from the innermost bracket outward, and simplify the RHS first: 176 ÷ (8 ÷ 2) = 176 ÷ 4 = 44. Now the LHS: (20 − x) stays as is for now → {37 − (20 − x)} = 17 + x → [42 − (17 + x)] = 25 − x → 57 − (25 − x) = 32 + x. Set equal to 44: 32 + x = 44 → x = 12. (Real TNPSC-style problem; this exact expression is a recurring coaching-site reference question.)

qa.question: Method — sum of two squares without brute-force squaring: Find 217 × 217 + 183 × 183.
qa.answer: Use a² + b² = (a+b)² − 2ab instead of squaring two 3-digit numbers by hand. Here a+b = 400 (round number — that's the clue this trick applies) and ab = 217 × 183 = 39,711. So a²+b² = 400² − 2(39,711) = 160,000 − 79,422 = 80,578. Recognizing 'the two numbers add to a round number' is the trigger for this shortcut.

qa.question: Method — difference of squares shortcut: Simplify (197 × 197 − 103 × 103) ÷ (197 − 103) without long multiplication.
qa.answer: a² − b² = (a+b)(a−b), so (a²−b²)/(a−b) = (a+b) directly — no multiplication needed at all. Here a=197, b=103, so the answer is simply 197+103 = 300. This 'difference of squares divided by the difference' shape is a favorite TNPSC trick question precisely because it looks like it needs heavy calculation but collapses to one addition.

qa.question: Method — fraction-of-decimal addition: Simplify 7/5 of 58 + 3/8 of 139.2.
Method — ratio substitution to simplify a compound ratio expression: If x : y = 3 : 4, find (4x + 5y) : (5x − 2y).
qa.answer: Don't solve for x and y separately — substitute x=3k, y=4k (k is just a scaling factor that will cancel). (4x+5y) = 12k+20k = 32k. (5x−2y) = 15k−8k = 7k. Ratio = 32k : 7k = 32 : 7. The k always cancels in a ratio question — that's the whole trick.

qa.question: Method — same ratio-substitution trick, different ratio: If x : y = 3 : 5, find (5x + 3y) : (15x − 2y).
Method — simultaneous equations feeding into a simplification target: If 3a + 4b = 22 and 8a − 5b = −4, find (a+b)².
qa.answer: Solve the pair normally: from 3a+4b=22 and 8a−5b=−4, elimination gives a=2, b=4 (check: 3(2)+4(4)=6+16=22 ✓; 8(2)−5(4)=16−20=−4 ✓). The question doesn't ask for a and b individually — it asks for (a+b)² = (2+4)² = 36. TNPSC often hides the real target ((a+b)², not a or b) at the end of a simultaneous-equation question — always re-read what's actually being asked before solving for individual variables.

qa.question: Method — mixed numbers with a nested fraction: Simplify 2½ + 3⅓ ÷ ⅔.
qa.answer: BODMAS still applies to fractions — division before addition. 3⅓ ÷ ⅔ = (10/3) ÷ (2/3) = (10/3) × (3/2) = 5. Then 2½ + 5 = 7½. The common mistake is adding 2½+3⅓ first — division always binds tighter than addition, fractions or not.

qa.question: Real exam (2017_q1 page 61 Q170): Simplify (2x⁴ − 162) / [(x² + 9)(2x − 6)].
qa.answer: Factor the numerator using difference of squares twice: 2x⁴−162 = 2(x⁴−81) = 2(x²−9)(x²+9) = 2(x−3)(x+3)(x²+9). The denominator is (x²+9)(2x−6) = (x²+9)×2×(x−3). Cancel the common (x²+9), the common 2, and the common (x−3) from top and bottom, leaving just (x+3) — the exam's marked answer.
qa.tip: Whenever you see a 4th-power term minus a perfect square number (like 162=2×81=2×9²), suspect a hidden 'difference of squares, applied twice' factoring — pull out the common factor first, then factor repeatedly rather than trying to divide the whole polynomial directly.

qa.question: Method — sum of cubes shortcut: Simplify (5³ + 3³) ÷ (5 + 3) without adding the cubes directly.
qa.answer: Use a³+b³ = (a+b)(a²−ab+b²), so (a³+b³)/(a+b) = a²−ab+b² directly. Here a=5, b=3: 25−15+9 = 19. (Check by brute force: 125+27=152, and 152/8=19 ✓.)

qa.question: Method — continued/nested fraction: Simplify 2 + 1/(3 + 1/4).
qa.answer: Work from the innermost fraction outward: 3 + 1/4 = 13/4. Then 1/(13/4) = 4/13. Finally, 2 + 4/13 = 30/13 (or 2 4/13 as a mixed number). Nested fractions must always be resolved innermost-first, exactly like nested brackets in BODMAS.

qa.question: Method — LCM-based addition/subtraction of unlike fractions: Simplify 3/4 + 5/6 − 1/3.
qa.answer: LCM of 4, 6, 3 is 12. Convert: 3/4=9/12, 5/6=10/12, 1/3=4/12. Sum: 9/12+10/12−4/12 = 15/12 = 5/4 (= 1¼). Always convert every fraction to the SAME denominator (the LCM) before adding or subtracting — never combine numerators across different denominators.

qa.question: Method — surd (square root) simplification: Simplify √72 + √8 − √18.
qa.answer: Break each surd into a perfect-square factor times a smaller surd: √72=√(36×2)=6√2, √8=√(4×2)=2√2, √18=√(9×2)=3√2. Now they share the same √2 'unit' and combine like algebra terms: 6√2+2√2−3√2 = 5√2.
qa.tip: Treat √2 (or whatever surd remains after simplifying) exactly like a variable, e.g. 'x' — 6x+2x−3x=5x is the same arithmetic as 6√2+2√2−3√2=5√2. Surds can only be combined once they share the same root part.

questions.explanation: Using a²−b² = (a+b)(a−b), dividing by (a−b) leaves just (a+b) = 425+375 = 800.

questions.prompt: If 5a + 2b = 16 and 3a − 4b = −6, find the value of (a+b)².
questions.explanation: Multiply the first equation by 2: 10a+4b=32. Add to the second (3a−4b=−6): 13a=26 → a=2. Substitute back: 5(2)+2b=16 → b=3. (a+b)²=(2+3)²=25.

questions.prompt: A trader borrows ₹16,000 at 12% p.a. simple interest and another ₹12,000 at 18% p.a. simple interest. In how many years will the two amounts owed (principal + interest) become equal?
questions.explanation: This mirrors a real 2025 TNPSC Aptitude question style (same numbers, Simple Interest unit) — included here as a reminder that 'Simplification' skills (careful fraction/ratio handling) carry directly into other Aptitude sub-topics like Simple Interest. Set 16000+16000(0.12)t = 12000+12000(0.18)t and solve for t to get 16⅔ years.
This is the (a+b)² pattern in disguise: a²+2ab+b² = (a+b)². Here a=89, b=11, so the answer is (89+11)² = 100² = 10,000 — no separate multiplication needed.

questions.prompt: Simplify (2x⁴ − 32) / [(x² + 4)(2x − 4)] — same method as a real 2017 TNPSC question, fresh numbers.
questions.explanation: 2x⁴−32 = 2(x⁴−16) = 2(x²−4)(x²+4) = 2(x−2)(x+2)(x²+4). Denominator = (x²+4)×2×(x−2). Cancelling the common (x²+4), 2, and (x−2) leaves (x+2).
questions.trick: Same shape as the real exam question: a 4th-power-minus-a-perfect-square numerator over a matching bracket pair always collapses via double difference-of-squares factoring.

questions.explanation: a³+b³=(a+b)(a²−ab+b²), so dividing by (a+b) leaves a²−ab+b² = 36−24+16 = 28.
questions.trick: Never add the cubes directly if you're then just going to divide by (a+b) — jump straight to a²−ab+b².
Resolve nested fractions innermost-first, exactly like nested BODMAS brackets.
Convert every fraction to the LCM denominator before combining numerators — never mix denominators.
Break each surd into (largest perfect square factor) × (remaining surd) first — then combine like algebraic terms.
Resolve exponents and multiplication/division left-to-right before any addition/subtraction — a classic BODMAS-order check.

questions.prompt: Find the value of 84² − 16² using the difference-of-squares identity.
questions.trick: Any 'square minus square' expression collapses to (sum)×(difference) — almost always faster than squaring both numbers separately.
